title: Ray diagrams
description: Given rays of light incident on spherical mirrors, let's practice drawing the reflected rays.

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror parallel to its principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A concave mirror converges rays parallel to the principal axis, at a single point.

[[☃ image 1]]

Rays incident parallel to the principal axis on a concave mirror pass through the focus, $\text F$, after reflection.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror after passing through its centre of curvature $\text C$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A line joining the centre of curvature $\text C$ and any point on a spherical mirror is normal to the mirror. An incident ray along this line will be normally incident on the mirror.

A ray that is incident normally on a mirror has zero angle of incidence. 

So, for an incident ray through $\text C$, the angle of incidence is zero, or $i=0$.
Since $i=0$ for an incident ray through $\text C$, angle of reflection must be zero too ($r=i=0$). So the ray will be reflected back in the opposite direction, through $\text C$.

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror after passing through its focus $\text F$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A concave mirror converges incoming rays that are parallel to the principal axis, at its focus.  

[[☃ image 1]]

But the opposite must be true too, since the reflected angle for a ray always equals its incident angle.

For a ray incident on a concave mirror through the focus $\text F$, the reflected ray emerges parallel to the principal axis.

[[☃ image 2]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror at the pole, $\text{P}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The line joining the point of incidence and the centre of curvature $\text C$, is the normal for a spherical mirror. Let's apply the law of reflection, $i=r$, for a ray incident at the pole $\text P$.

If a ray is incident at $\text P$, the normal is the line connecting $\text P$ and $\text C$ - which is the same as the principal axis! So the reflected ray must be on the other side of the principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror parallel to its principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A convex mirror diverges a beam of rays parallel to the principal axis, away from a single point.

[[☃ image 1]]

Incident rays parallel to the principal axis of a convex mirror appear to emerge from the focus $\text F$ (located behind the mirror) after reflection.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror in the direction towards its centre of curvature $\text C$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A line joining the centre of curvature $\text C$ and any point on a spherical mirror is normal to the mirror. A ray that is incident along this line will be incident normally on the mirror.

A ray that is incident normally on a mirror has zero angle of incidence. 

So, for a ray incident on a convex mirror towards $\text C$, the angle of incidence is zero, or $i=0$.
Since $i=0$ for an incident ray towards $\text C$, angle of reflection must be zero too ($r=i=0$). So the ray will be reflected back in the opposite direction, through $\text C$.

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror in the direction towards its focus $\text F$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A convex mirror diverges incoming rays that are parallel to the principal axis, away from its focus.  

[[☃ image 1]]

But the opposite must be true too, since the reflected angle for a ray always equals its incident angle.

For a ray incident on a convex mirror in the direction towards the focus, the reflected ray emerges parallel to the principal axis.

[[☃ image 2]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a spherical mirror at the pole, $\text P$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following diagrams shows the reflected ray correctly?**  
*Note: The smooth side is the reflecting part of the mirror, and the dotted side is the back.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The line joining the point of incidence and the centre of curvature $\text C$, is the normal for a spherical mirror. Let's apply the law of reflection, $i=r$, for a ray incident at the pole $\text P$.

If a ray is incident at $\text P$, the normal is the line connecting $\text P$ and $\text C$ - which is the same as the principal axis! So the reflected ray must be on the other side of the principal axis.

[[☃ image 2]]

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]
